body_txt: Culturally, we associate the Moon with nighttime, due to it being overall the brightest celestial body in the night sky, especially when it's full. This has led to a visual shorthand in media where the Moon is shown only at night, and every night. It is also usually either bright and full or a clear, neat crescent, even many nights in a row. In reality, the difference in time the Moon takes to trace a circuit across the sky (twenty-eight days versus the Sun's year) means that it's primarily in the sky during the day for about half of a given month, and as an orbiting object, it also cannot maintain a static position on one side of its parent body anyway. It's just a lot less noticeable in those circumstances because the Sun outshines it by a factor of several hundred thousand. Thus, because it's noticed less often and stands out less, we don't strongly associate it with the daytime sky. In mythology and in fantasy worldbuilding, this sometimes leads to a contrast between solar and lunar personifications or deities, often centering their dynamics around an inability to meet or share the sky except during rare events such as eclipses or a willing separation between the two. In video games in particular, the Moon's representation is even stranger. Especially in 3D games where the player can look up at any time, it's very convenient to represent an In-Universe Game Clock by having the sun trace a distinct arc across the sky, just as it does in real life. But when night arrives, it's common to portray the Moon as a sort of anti-Sun: it rises in the same place, follows the exact same path note the moon's actual path differs from the Sun's by up to five degrees , and may take the same amount of time to pass, depending on how the day/night cycle is regulated. It may be possible to see the Sun and Moon at the same time during dawn or dusk, as one is rising and the other is setting. It's also common to depict them at the same size, which is more or less accurate, although the actual Moon's apparent size varies slightly over time. This arrangement has a number of practical benefits for players. It makes it easy to track the progress of time in the game by tracking a celestial body, the moonlight can justify being able to see at night, and using the same paths for the sun and moon can reduce strain on some lighting engines, since they don't have to calculate shadow angles for more than one source. Often the amount of light is exaggerated to being the same as the sun, but with a slightly blue filter on it. As such, it can be considered an Acceptable Break From Reality as long as the story isn't presenting itself as being realistic. In these settings, eclipses are often perceived as unnatural and frightening events, due to being a meeting of things that naturally should never meet, and a sign that the cosmic order is going out of balance. More rarely, usually if the Sun and Moon are highly personified, they might be depicted as a rare chance for the two to meet and be together. Not that, in and of itself, a single depiction of a night Moon may or may not mean anything. When a story continues over several nights, and the Moon is always present at night and only ever present at night, then things are being simplified (or the writer just neglected to do astronomy research). Subtrope of Artistic License – Space . For examples pertaining only to the moon's phase and not any other peculiarities of its behavior, see Frequently Full Moon . For examples where the relative lengths of day and night do not change, see Eternal Equinox . Compare Moonburn , when characters can be burned, or believe that they can be burned, by excessive exposure to moonlight.
